title: Steering the Ship
subtitle: The risk of going the wrong way

hook.metaphor: You can run a ship flawlessly — engine humming, crew drilled, decks spotless — and still sail it confidently in the wrong direction, straight onto the rocks. Strategic risk is the risk of the *direction* being wrong, not the operations. It's the most dangerous risk of all because everything can look perfectly well-run right up to the moment the whole voyage proves pointless.
hook.scenario: A company is brilliantly run — efficient, compliant, low on operational incidents. But it makes a product the world is rapidly abandoning, and leadership keeps optimising it instead of changing course. Operationally flawless, strategically doomed. Which risk is about to sink it?

learn: **Strategic risk is the risk that your big-picture choices — what business to be in, which markets, which bets — turn out wrong**, threatening your fundamental objectives and survival. It's about *direction*, not execution.

It's the odd one out among risks because it's about doing the *wrong thing well* rather than doing the right thing badly. Our company has near-zero operational, compliance, and even financial-control problems — and is still doomed, because the *strategy* is wrong. Polishing the brass on a sinking course doesn't help.

Strategic risk is uniquely dangerous for three reasons: it's **slow and quiet** (no alarm goes off when your market is dying), it's **existential** (it threatens the whole organisation, not one process), and it's often **invisible from inside** good operational metrics — you can be hitting every KPI on the way to irrelevance (Kodak, Blockbuster, Nokia).

It's also the risk most tied to **opportunity**: strategy is about taking bets to win, so strategic risk and strategic *reward* are two sides of one coin. The goal isn't to avoid strategic risk (that means never moving) but to take the *right* bets with eyes open.

And it sits at the top of the risk hierarchy: operational, credit, and market risks are about running the chosen course well; strategic risk asks whether the course itself is right. Which is why it's fundamentally the **board's** responsibility (a later level).
bridge: So at work, you can hold a question that day-to-day metrics never ask: *are we doing the right thing, or just doing the wrong thing efficiently?* A flawless operation pointed in a dying direction is the classic strategic-risk trap — no operational dashboard will warn you, because every gauge reads green right up until the market disappears beneath you.

interactions.title: Flawless but doomed
interactions.prompt: A superbly-run company keeps optimising a product the world is abandoning. Which risk threatens it?

options.label: Operational risk — something must be breaking.
options.feedback: Nothing operational is breaking — that's the trap. The company executes brilliantly. The danger is the *direction*: a dying product. That's strategic risk, invisible to operational metrics.

options.label: Strategic risk — the direction is wrong, however well it's executed.
options.feedback: Exactly. It's doing the wrong thing well. Strategic risk threatens the whole voyage regardless of operational excellence — Kodak and Blockbuster ran fine right up to irrelevance.

options.label: No real risk — it's well-run, so it's safe.
options.feedback: 'Well-run' only covers execution. A perfectly-run company sailing toward a vanishing market is in the gravest danger of all — strategic, and unflagged by any operational gauge.

interactions.title: Strategic or operational?
interactions.prompt: Sort each issue by whether it's about the direction (strategic) or running it well (operational).

items.label: Betting the company on a declining product
Entering a market that doesn't want you
A staff member making a data-entry error
A server running out of capacity

teachBack: Explain it to a friend in one line: “Strategic risk is sailing the wrong direction flawlessly — the risk that your big-picture bets are wrong — and it's the deadliest because every operational gauge reads green right up to the moment the market disappears.”

checkpoint.explain: It's about the big-picture choices being wrong, regardless of how well they're executed.

checkpoint.q: Why is strategic risk so dangerous?
options: It's slow, existential, and invisible to good operational metrics
checkpoint.explain: You can hit every KPI on the road to irrelevance — no operational alarm warns of a dying direction.

checkpoint.q: The goal with strategic risk is to…
options: Avoid all bets and never change
Take the right bets with eyes open — it's tied to opportunity
Ignore it as the board's hobby
checkpoint.explain: Strategy means taking bets to win; the aim is wise, eyes-open risk-taking, not paralysis.
